context: Common neighborhood label used in bus-stop names (○○駅前) and small-business signs. Indicates the immediate plaza in front of the station.
Directional label used in transit-system signage to indicate retail / facilities inside the paid area of a station building.

gloss_en: North station / North exit station name
context: Many cities use compass-direction prefixes for multi-station hubs (きた駅 = north station). Compare to みなみ駅 / ひがし駅 / にし駅.
Sign attached to the gate of every elementary school in Japan. Often paired with the school's name on a wooden / brass plaque.
Sign attached to the gate of every middle school in Japan. Uses 中 (middle / inside) + 学校 (school).
Generic school signage — common on school-zone road signs and on the front gate of any educational facility.
Generic company-building label — common suffix on corporate signage (○○会社) and in office-park directories.
Suffix on the original / flagship location of a chain restaurant or retail brand. Contrasted with 支店 (branch) in chain-store signage.
Bookstore signage — small independent shops often write 本や rather than the kanji-only 本屋 (屋 is N4). The kana 'や' is common in shop names.
Phrasing seen on used-bookstore shopfronts in Jimbocho (Tokyo's book-district). Conveys 'second-hand books'.
Single-character signage on street-name plates (○○道 = the X road). Common in older neighborhood signage.
Hiking-trail signage in mountain parks — wooden post markers with carved 山道 indicating a footpath.
Single-character signage on bridge nameplates and river-bank parks (○○川 = X river). Common in landscape signage.
Schedule / hours label on shop signs and bus timetables (午前10時 = 10 AM). Contrasted with 午後 (afternoon).
Schedule / hours label on shop signs and bus timetables (午後5時まで = until 5 PM). Pair with 午前 in any time-window signage.
Restaurant-board prefix used for daily specials (今日のおすすめ = today's recommendation). Also common on bulletin-board headers (今日の天気 = today's weather).
Service-availability label on shop signage (毎日 = open daily). Often paired with hours (毎日10時から).
Price-tag adjective used on hand-written menu boards or discount-shop labels to indicate higher-tier items.
Price-tag adjective used on hand-written menu boards and discount-shop banners (安い! 100円!). The same kanji 安 also means 'safe' in 安全.
Form-field label used on every clinic / city-hall / school registration form. The honorific お is standard on form labels.
Ticket-machine and form-field label (受付番号 / 整理番号). Also on bus-stop sequence labels (○番のりば = bay number X).
Polite-form label seen above water dispensers in restaurants, public buildings, and on water-fountain plaques. The honorific お softens the request to a friendly offer.
Price-tag denomination seen on 100-yen-shop banners and bargain-bin labels (the ubiquitous 百均 / 100-yen store culture).
Price tag at the next-up denomination. Common on bento-box prices and small-purchase ATM withdrawal prompts (千円札 = 1000-yen note).
Major-purchase denomination — the largest single Japanese banknote. Seen on cash-deposit ATM screens and high-tier menu/sake-bottle prices.
Menu-board quantity label — '1 portion for 1 person'. Often paired with prices (一人前 1,200円) for set meals.
Family-size or shareable-portion label — common on takeout-bento promotional banners (三人前 2,800円).
Month header on calendars, magazine covers, and seasonal-promotion signs. Read with on-yomi いち + がつ. Note: 1月 (Arabic numeral) is equally common.
April marks the start of the Japanese fiscal/school year — 4月 banners on new-employee welcome signs, school-entrance ceremonies, cherry-blossom-season menus.
May features Golden Week 5月の連休 — multiple national holidays. Travel-promotion signs and koinobori (carp-streamer) displays carry this date prominently.
Rainy-season starts in June — 6月の梅雨 forecasts on weather-board displays. Also seen on rainy-season umbrella-shop promotional banners.
Summer-festival 七夕 (Tanabata) on July 7 is heavily signed — 7月7日 wishes on bamboo decorations in shops and stations.
August = obon-festival month (お盆) — train-station signs announce holiday-period schedules; 8月のお盆 fireworks-festival banners.
September starts autumn — autumn-foods menu rotations and 9月の秋祭り (autumn-festival) banners replace summer signage.
Read じゅうがつ (irregular contraction of じゅう + がつ → not じゅうげつ). October hosts Sports Day 体育の日 and harvest-festival signs.
Day-of-week schedule label on shop signs (火曜日定休 = closed Tuesdays) and clinic-hours boards. Read with on-yomi か (fire) + よう + び.
Tree-themed day label. Common on rotating-special menus (木曜日のおすすめ = Thursday's recommendation) and weekly farmer's-market schedules.
End-of-workweek day — 金曜日のハッピーアワー (Friday happy hour) on izakaya promotional signs; also payday-related (金 = money).
Weekend day label on store-hours signs (土曜日10:00-20:00) and family-event banners. Most retail and dining establishments operate full hours on Saturdays.
Seasonal promotional banner prefix — 今年もよろしくお願いします (this year too, please favor us). Common on New Year's shopfront greetings.
Quick-service signage (10分でカット = 10-minute haircut) and short-wait time indicators on station departure boards.
Casual time-of-day label on shop signs — 8時半から (from 8:30) is common on morning-hours boards. The kanji 半 = half, paired with 時 to mean 'half-hour past'.
Kids' section / children's products label. The hiragana ども-form is preferred over kanji 子供 on consumer-facing signage to soften the tone for parents.
Student-discount sign at museums and theaters (学生 800円 = student 800 yen); also used on student-ID labels (学生証).
Faculty-office nameplate on school doors (○○先生); also addresses doctors at clinics (山田先生). The 先生 honorific is used for any expert-recognition role.
Friendship-themed retail signage — 友だち と一緒に (with friends) on group-discount banners at restaurants and entertainment venues.
Department-store seasonal promotion banner (third Sunday of June). 父の日プレゼント = Father's Day gift display tables.
Department-store seasonal promotion banner (second Sunday of May). 母の日に贈る花 = flowers to give on Mother's Day, common florist signage.
中国 = China — common prefix on restaurant signs 中国料理 (Chinese cuisine) and tourism-info boards. Reads ちゅうごく; 中 is also Japan's western Chuugoku region.
School-classification label — 私立 (private) vs 公立 (public, N4). The 私 (I / private) appears on school-gate plaques and admission-document headers.
Customer-facing question form on shop-hours notices (何時から何時まで = from what time until what time). Also common in casual conversation.
Month header. February features Setsubun (節分, beans-throwing day) and Valentine's Day — chocolate displays and seasonal-menu boards prominently show 2月.
Month header reading uses standard digit-chain rules (十一 = 11, がつ contracted). November = 七五三 (shichi-go-san) children's festival.
Year-end month — シーズン中 (peak season) for shopfront 十二月の福袋 (December lucky-bag) promotions, and year-end-cleaning service labels.
Day-of-week schedule label. Many businesses use 月曜日定休 (closed Mondays) on storefront notices to indicate weekly rest day.
Day-of-week schedule label. Used on mid-week event banners and 水曜日のお買い得 (Wednesday's bargain) supermarket promotions.
Day-of-week schedule label. 日曜日も営業 (open Sundays too) is a common shopfront marker in commercial districts.
Service-availability label (毎週月曜日 = every Monday). Common on workshop / class schedule boards and recurring-event signage.
Language label seen on language-school signs, tourist-information boards (日本語のみ = Japanese only), and software-language selectors.
Sign on staff-only doors and information-desk signage at train stations. 駅員 means JR/metro employee — typically the person to ask for help.
Sign distinguishing customer area from staff area. 店員さん = the shop assistant; 店員募集 (now hiring) is a frequent help-wanted banner.
Membership-club label. 会員のみ (members only) on premium-area entrances; 会員カード on signup-form labels.
Single-character directional sign on stair signage and floor-indicator panels. Often paired with arrow (↑) to mean 'upstairs' or 'upper floor'.
Single-character directional sign on stair signage. Paired with arrow (↓) to indicate 'downstairs' or 'lower floor' — opposite of 上.
Tourist-information label (外国の方 = foreign visitors) and form-field marker (外国人登録 = foreign resident registration).
Station exit signage (compass-direction labels on every major station). 東口 = east exit; pair with 西口/南口/北口 to specify which side of the station.
Compass-direction station exit. Tokyo's Shinjuku 新宿西口 is the iconic west-exit example — major bus terminus + office-tower district.
Compass-direction station exit. 南口 typically leads to bus terminals or commercial districts; pair with the station name on guide signs.
Compass-direction station exit. 北口 is one of the fundamental wayfinding labels in any large Japanese station.
Duration label on parking-meter signs (一時間 500円 = 500 yen per hour), service-time indicators (一時間以内 = within one hour), and rental-by-the-hour boards.
Most common surname on doorplate signs (表札 / hyoosatsu). 田 (rice field) + 中 (middle) — literally 'middle of the rice field'. About 1.3 million people share this name.
Common surname on doorplate signs. 山 (mountain) + 本 (origin/main) — literally 'base of the mountain'. Frequent in central / western Japan.
Spring promotion banner (花見特集 / 花見弁当). 花見 is the iconic spring activity — picnic under cherry blossoms; signage peaks in late March / early April.
Weather-forecast board label (今日の空 = today's sky). Also seen on rooftop-bar signs and airline branding (空 = sky in 空港 airport, though 港 itself is N4).
Newsstand and convenience-store label. 新聞 = newspaper; major dailies (朝日新聞 / 読売新聞) are displayed under this category sign.
Library / quiet-zone label. 読書スペース (reading space) on bookstore signs and 読書の秋 (reading-autumn) seasonal-promotion campaigns.
Cultural-class signage. 書道教室 (calligraphy class) outside community centers and stationery shops selling brushes + ink.
Forward-looking promotion banner — 来月オープン (opening next month) on under-construction shop signs; also on event-announcement posters.
Shopfront closure notice — 本日休み (closed today) on dangling cardboard signs. Distinct from 定休日 (fixed closure day, slightly more formal).
Comment-box header on customer-feedback forms (お客様の一言 = customer's word). Also on bulletin-board comment-card labels.
Title plaque on the station-master's office door. The 駅長 is the senior official at each station; their announcement carries authority on platform notices.
Restaurant menu category label. 白米 = plain white rice — the default rice option, contrasted with 玄米 (brown rice, 玄 is N4) on healthier menu boards.
Accessibility-service label at public-facility information desks (手話サービス = sign-language service available). 手 (hand) + 話 (speak) = literal meaning.
Single-character optician / eye-care label. 目 (eye) on glasses-shop signs and eye-clinic logos. Also appears in 目薬 (eye drops; 薬 is N4, so use 目 alone on signage).
Single-character gym / fitness label. 力 appears on weightlifting / strength-training signs and 力こぶ (biceps muscle) cartoon mascots in workout-related signage.
Destination label on intercity bus departure boards and commuter train head-signs. The 行 (read ゆき in this context) marks 'bound for' — 大阪行 / 京都行 follow the same pattern.
Action-button label on e-commerce checkout screens (今すぐ買う = buy now). Also on in-store self-service kiosks and vending-machine prompts.
Public-fountain and park-water-tap label. 飲み水 explicitly marks water as safe to drink (vs irrigation or decorative water). Common at trail heads and park entrances.
